one_line_takeaway: KryptoPilot shows that open-world, fine-grained knowledge retrieval plus reasoning/resource governance—rather than a stronger base model—is what lets an LLM agent reliably automate high-difficulty cryptographic CTF exploitation (56-60% on NYU-CTF, 100% on InterCode, 79% in live competitions).
key_finding: On InterCode-CTF (18 crypto challenges, after excluding 1 of 19 for a dead remote), KryptoPilot solves 100% (18/18) vs. CTFAgent 83% and Plain-Agent 72%. On the harder NYU-CTF (52 challenges) it solves 29/52 = 56% (60% = 31/52 with HeavyThink) vs. CTFAgent 21/52 = 40% (8 more challenges solved). Ablations: removing Deep Research drops NYU solve rate to 50% (26/52); removing Model Router leaves solve rate unchanged (still 18/18 on InterCode) but raises avg solving time from 30.39s to 168.94s (>5x) and token cost from $0.083 to $0.114, showing the router is an efficiency (not accuracy) mechanism. On the 21 NYU challenges both frameworks solve, KryptoPilot averages 526.1s vs. CTFAgent 566.5s. In six live 2025 competitions it solved 26/33 crypto challenges (79%), with 100% at four events (XCTF Finals, Qiangwang Challenge, N1CTF Junior, QnQSec), 57% at RCTF, and 33% (2/6) at HITCON, including multiple first-solve and one solo-solve instance. The exploratory study (Table 1) grounds the thesis: full-paper knowledge raises code correctness dramatically (e.g., babyrsa 0%->83%, Check-in 0%->28%, LinearCasino 60%->100%) where abstracts alone often fail or even mislead.
core_contribution: The paper introduces KryptoPilot, the authors' own open-world knowledge-augmented LLM agent framework for automated cryptographic exploitation in Capture-the-Flag (CTF) settings. Its central claim, supported by an exploratory study (Section 3.1, Table 1), is a diagnostic reframing: failures of existing LLM agents on high-difficulty crypto CTF challenges stem primarily from insufficient knowledge granularity and executability, NOT from limited model reasoning capacity. To act on this, KryptoPilot contributes three coordinated subsystems around a central reasoning unit: (1) a Knowledge Subsystem with a Deep Research (DR) pipeline for dynamic open-world retrieval of long-form primary sources (papers, blogs, GitHub reference implementations) plus a persistent workspace ('process-once, reuse-many' via doc_id); (2) a Reasoning/Tool Subsystem integrating a SageMath backend (sage-ctf-docker); and (3) a Governance Subsystem doing behavioral governance via prompt-engineered SOPs (biasing toward vetted third-party libraries over ad-hoc reimplementation) and resource governance via dynamic tiered Model Routing (L1-L5 difficulty self-assessment routing cheap vs. expensive models). Note: CTFAgent (Ji et al. [58]), Plain-Agent (Turtayev et al. [68]), the Deep Research paradigm [56], RAG [60], and benchmarks InterCode/NYU-CTF are cited prior work used as baselines/tools, NOT this paper's contributions.
threat_model: Offensive/dual-use security. The system is an autonomous LLM agent acting as the attacker: given a cryptographic CTF challenge (source code and a live service), it performs reconnaissance, models the cryptographic weakness (e.g., truncated linear recurrences / Hidden Number Problem solved via lattice reduction, RSA flaws, PRNG bit-leakage, AES-CTR+zlib), fetches cryptanalytic knowledge from the open web/academic repos/GitHub, generates and executes exploit code (SageMath, pwntools) against local and remote targets, and recovers the flag. Capabilities assumed: internet/tool access, code execution, and paid access to top-tier models. The paper frames challenges as inspired by real deployed-system vulnerabilities (CVE-2014-0160 Heartbleed, CVE-2017-15361 ROCA, CVE-2022-21449), and explicitly claims generalization to real vulnerability discovery/exploitation beyond crypto (web, reverse engineering, blockchain), making it an automated-exploitation capability paper rather than a defensive one. No attacker-vs-defender adversarial game is formalized; the 'defense' side is only the fairness filtering that prevents the agent from retrieving verbatim writeups.
limitations: Authors' own RQ5 failure analysis (NYU-CTF, within 50-round budget): failures are attributed not to reasoning depth or interaction budget but to (1) incorrect attack-paradigm identification—interactive-oracle/protocol/implementation-flaw challenges misread as static crypto problems; (2) weak multi-stage attack planning (localized single-stage edits, low-information-gain loops); (3) poor handling of statistical/cross-round accumulative attacks (assumes deterministic single-interaction feedback); and (4) inability to execute the final counterintuitive parameter-choice step that needs experiential heuristics. They state simply adding rounds will not fix this. Reviewer-noted caveats from the text: code correctness is scored by an LLM-as-a-judge; the exploratory study is small (9 challenges, single-round, ChatGPT-5.1); Plain-Agent could not run on NYU-CTF (implementation coupled to InterCode), so that baseline is only on the easy benchmark where all three methods score high; and heavy reliance on GPT-5.1 Thinking / GPT-OSS-120B plus paid search services conditions the results. HITCON solve rate is only 33% (2/6).

datasets_used: InterCode-CTF (crypto subset, 18 of 19 challenges used; built from picoCTF)
NYU-CTF / NYU-CTF-Bench (crypto subset, 52 challenges; from CSAW CTF 2017-2023)
Six live 2025 CTF competitions (XCTF International Finals, Qiangwang Challenge on Cyber Mimic Defense Qual., N1CTF Junior, QnQSec CTF, HITCON CTF 2025, RCTF 2025) — 33 crypto challenges total
Exploratory study set: 9 representative high-difficulty crypto CTF challenges (Table 1)
quantitative_results: InterCode-CTF: KryptoPilot 100% (18/18) vs CTFAgent 83% vs Plain-Agent 72%
NYU-CTF: KryptoPilot 56% (29/52); HeavyThink 60% (31/52); w/o Deep Research 50% (26/52); CTFAgent 40% (21/52)
Live competitions: 26/33 crypto challenges solved (79% overall); 100% at 4 of 6 events; RCTF 57% (4/7); HITCON 33% (2/6)
Model Router ablation (InterCode): solve rate unchanged (18/18) but avg time 30.39s -> 168.94s (>5x) and cost $0.083 -> $0.114 when removed
NYU-CTF avg time on 21 jointly-solved challenges: KryptoPilot 526.1s vs CTFAgent 566.5s
Exploratory study code-correctness gains from full-paper knowledge, e.g. babyrsa 0%->83%, LinearCasino 60%->100%, Check-in 0%->28%, MyCurveErrorLearn 0%->50%
Efficiency vs baselines on InterCode: avg solving time ~10% of Plain-Agent and ~21% of CTFAgent; monetary cost ~34% of Plain-Agent and ~72% of CTFAgent
baselines_compared: CTFAgent (Ji et al. [58], two-stage RAG + environment augmentation)
Plain-Agent (Turtayev et al. [68], ReAct&Plan mode; InterCode only)
Internal ablations: KryptoPilot w/o Deep Research, KryptoPilot w/o Model Router, KryptoPilot + HeavyThink
Exploratory-study conditions: LLM-only vs +abstract vs +full paper (ChatGPT-5.1)
